sentences.text: Well, that relationship is well one of the, if not the most not only pleasant but-- but penetrating episode and time in my life, to meeting-- meeting this-- this girl.
I was determined if I can get her into my-- into my net, and into a promise, and so on, it's worthwhile to die for her.
Die with her, because we are in it.
Die together.
Or as-- as aside, what will come, and personal feeling, and so to die for her.
And the real time we had together about three months on and off, because they had their own special training that trains.
Also they went to different courses, but to same place, but different times, and different instructors, and so on to the same army barrack.
Once we crossed each other there, but we-- we didn't even stop, you see.
We just walked by each other.
That's how it was.
But we visited really pleasant places like, like the-- the Cairo opera, where King Farouk.
And he was-- he was a big bellied [INAUDIBLE] king.
But the wife was a beautiful, beautiful woman.
My god.
We were just right sitting in the-- the first-- first seats in the row where they walked by us to the reserved seats and so on.
We visited the pyramids.
But-- and there is a huge, huge park there-- a zoo and everything else.
There were the-- there were the elephants and giraffes.
And so around free-- free, like-- like being-- being out there, this is so huge.
The Garden of Sun was the name of it, right on the-- right on the Nile-- Nile River.
And once walking there, the kids there, all begging.
They just little kids.
And there, and not far from-- from the park, there were no [INAUDIBLE] coming out with [INAUDIBLE] hand, you see.
And she just simply emptied out her pockets to the-- to-- to the kids.
She was so-- so really loving heart, and you see, to watch it is humane, real humane.
So we were how are you going to get home?
How are you going to buy-- buy streetcar to get home? 'Oh, you're going to carry me home.' OK.
[LAUGHS] Simple as that, she has this-- this characteristic that I-- I didn't find out half for it.
Only when I read her diary then.
That was who she-- who she really was.
Did she write you any poems?
No, no, no.
And only-- only after the war, when here in-- in Toronto through Rabbi [?
Sagem. ?] Have you ever heard of him?
He's a Hungarian-- Hungarian rabbi.
And he visited Miss Szenes and-- and Julie there several-- several times.
And then when-- when he came back, I already-- I already had the book to English of her diary.
And Rabbi [?
Sagem ?] came me.
He gave me some-- some of the smaller, smaller, smaller books what was written in-- in-- in Israel, and-- and some in Hungary.
But never a full story about-- about her.
So I-- I-- I really couldn't find-- find out the whole thing.
But then when I was then introduced to Rabbi [?
Sagem, ?] he took that news.
If they'd ever heard about me, [INAUDIBLE] or George.
And the only thing that they could remember, because there the-- the postal service was-- was really bad.
And they were never allowed to-- to-- to correspond.
And anyway, but there were what you call runners between Cairo and-- Cairo Palestine, so.
And all that they-- they remembered, the mother and then the brother, that there was one.
There was one occasion when one-- one of the-- of the messengers dropped in one word from-- from Hannah that she had-- she sent this message.
Finally I had-- I had found somebody.
And that was all.
And [INAUDIBLE] also has a [INAUDIBLE], because they were also so backward.
They came from Romania and Slovakia, and so.
But, you know, they lived in fascist countries, and so on.
And they were all communists.
But what would you know?
What literature you were-- you were able to-- to read under the fascist press for decades of [INAUDIBLE].
And that's why-- that's why she finally made her decision.
Well, he's [? demented ?] for [INAUDIBLE] saw all in-- in her-- in her the diary.
She talks about it.
The boys and boys and boys I bought.
But I just I didn't find the one yet.
And luckily, and-- and to my great joy, I-- I-- I was the one.
Would have been survived together.
Sometimes I doubt it.
Because somehow I felt that spiritually she was above me.
She was a-- a-- a--a real-- a real-- a real genius.
If you read her-- her diary, you would-- you would find out.
But then this is something, that something happened then, when we coming back from a suburban resort where you had a swimming pool.
We took a long walk back.
And then we started to talk about our-- our future, that-- and to the point that-- that when we survive, if you survive, we're going to go back to-- come back to Canada for-- for a while.
I have to-- to go back anyway to be disarmed or discharged and so on.
And I have duties to do there, and so on.
And she had a sense already that Hungary's gone a bit different country.
She-- she was so convinced that Hungary is going to be liberated by the Red Army.
And on the wake of that liberation that we have different-- different Hungary.
And then we going to go back to [INAUDIBLE] I belong here, too, because of my-- my principles.
Very, very, very, very little.
Very little.
And we talked-- the only thing that came out from her that she was-- she was in contact with the-- with the workers' youth movement.
And then she didn't have to spell it out that she was a young-- young communist.
But at the same time, at the same time, she had a few words about Zionist, that there were Zionists among the-- and in Hungary.
I think, as you know there were three [? realms. ?] And everyone-- the working class, and the middle class, and the high class-- the Zionists.
Like-- like [?
Kostner ?] was the-- was the top [INAUDIBLE] So that's how-- that's hpw the times-- how the times passed.
And there was no movie.
I don't think we ever attended a movie.
But than we met in [?
Groppe. ?] [?
Groppe ?] was a-- a coffeehouse sort of, and a pub, and so on.
The coffee house was-- was separate from-- from the-- from the-- from the pub where you could consume.
And well she-- she tasted some [INAUDIBLE] Algerian wine.
Good.
It was really strong wine, Algerian-- Algerian wine there.
But we didn't fraternize very much, because at that one time we noticed that my friend Adam and myself, we were alone at that time, and set the table.
And the other two guys sat to-- sat to a table.
And we discovered they were [INAUDIBLE] German spy.
With that-- that word, intimate.
Well that was the Shepherd's Hotel.
That was reserved for the-- for the Allied officers, who were 90% British officers.
So we could go there.
We could go there any time.
And finally.
Finally.
Well, we, yes, we went into it.
And I think it was about the third time when I rented a room.
And then it came-- the consummation.
Finally we arrived there.
But then in my book [LAUGHS] I also discussing it with the-- with the editor of the book.
And I took a look at it.
Tell me something, a nice phrase that I don't have to spell it out in a vulgar-- more vulgar way.
And he found it, this expression of consummation, [INAUDIBLE].
And he misunderstood me, that we never reached the point of a consummation.
And that's how put it though in a book.
And I have to correct it.
[INTERPOSING VOICES] --read the book and we were [INAUDIBLE] to describe together know it all.
But after all, she was a soldier also.
And well, you write, and it's a mistake in the book.
But I have to admit it that after all, well, we were young and healthy and-- and energetic, and so.
And finally.
But we were-- to make this remark as a write through that she was very rigid too, and that she was trembling more than before she made a parachute jump.
Because there-- there she was a daring I won't say hero, because we're on practice you are not-- not a hero yet.
But as I hear it, he was-- he was daring devil.
He always jumped the first, and when we was over, and then he indicated come on-- come on, follow me.
And it's true that that's how it happened.

And then when she had to go to the bed first, she was a little trembling, like a-- like a really [? donkey. ?] [LAUGHS] She didn't know what to expect, because she was-- you read it-- you read it in her-- in her diary how all the boys that were swarming around-- around her in the kibbutz.
And so many of them, mind you.
And some-- some were very serious, that in some-- some-- in some places she indicates in her-- in her diary she was almost raped by-- you could-- you could feel it.
And somehow because of this-- of this forcefulness.
So made-- made her more-- more rigid.
Because she-- she looked for things up here first.
And that's what she-- she wanted to be sure who the man is before she opens herself for physical.
Yes.
And yes.
But unfortunately all we had three times.
And then one day I was going up to the-- to the club.
And they told me, well, she's not in.
She's out somewhere.
So it was nothing [INAUDIBLE].
She might be called-- called in for-- for a briefing, or some kind of a quick lecture for some information, connection with the-- with the mission and so on.
When the second day she's not there.
Third day I went up and then I looked for the-- for the man who was-- who was the chief among them.
And among the-- well the 22, 7 of them were-- were Hungarians.
Most of them-- most of them from-- from Romania, from Transylvania, [INAUDIBLE].
And I ask him [INAUDIBLE].
Well look, she was-- she was called in.
She was called in.
And most likely said, well I think she's-- I think she's off.
And I knew that she was off.
Because we were on-- on-- on 24 hours readiness.
And they just called on when we were-- we were transferred from Cairo.
We had a notice about 10-- 10:15 in the morning that we'd be ready, completely ready for then to-- to board a jeep at 6 o'clock in the morning.
And we had to be ready.
And went and goodbye.
Oh well, quite a long time.
But we left Cairo in May, and she left at the end of May-- March.
Into Italy.
Then there in Italy, then in-- in-- in San Vito.
Brindisi was the-- Brindisi was the-- was the port.
We sailed.
We sailed from which?
That port where Suez?
No, no.
Port Said.
Yeah.
Port Said.
And then-- then we docked in-- in Haifa.
And we had about two hours.
So-- so we were disembarking to looked around a bit about Palestine.
So this is the land of hers.
And then we left--
Oh, well-- well we knew [INAUDIBLE] in the town when we met-- that she was from Palestine.
Because they had the same-- same insignia.
But you never talked about-- about they-- they training, the [? framing ?] caps, and so on.
But then the later I figured out that [INAUDIBLE] that that must have been the school for the-- for the secret agents there.
And they put us through training.
Well it was something, the [INAUDIBLE].
Have you ever heard of it?
Yes.
Well, I have a book in Hungarian.
Can you read it?
No.
Why?
[LAUGHTER] He talks about this [INAUDIBLE], how those things has happened there.
And-- and he also characterizes her as-- as-- a-- as a wild and restless and so on.
And he also makes a point when he finally-- finally met her-- met her in-- in Yugoslavia or something, she was a changed girl.
She was a changed girl.
Yeah.
Yeah.
Then I knew, well [INAUDIBLE] well, you don't know where it-- where it comes from, unfortunately.
But still she couldn't-- she couldn't reform.
She was so restless, and this was her fate.
She was so anxious to cross the border.
She was warned against it.
But she [INAUDIBLE] she crossed [INAUDIBLE] with two other Hungarian boys.
Well we were-- then we were shipped out by-- by ship into-- into-- into Italy, and then in San Vito.
It was about 20 miles north of Brindisi, where they had the report and the only airport.
And no, the port was Taranto, Taranto in the west coast of-- in-- in-- in--in Italy, in the boots, [LAUGHS] what you call it there.
And then Brindisi was the-- was an airport there.
And from-- from 20 miles north from there, San Vito, they expropriated a villa, a Italian who was a-- who was a Navy captain.
But then-- then he was away.
Somewhere he was a-- he was a prisoner of the-- of the Allies, of the-- of the British.
So that was the headquarters for this-- for this operation for the SOV.
And all those who went over the head of me where Hannah was also parachuted in from there into Yugoslavia from San Vito.
And we got there in-- in first weekend in May.
And then she parachuted into-- into Yugoslavia, because it was the Yugoslav Partisans, Tito's Partisan army was supplied by the British.
I remember this.
As a matter of fact, it was Churchill's plot who actually arranged the Yugoslav uprising.
And he was the one who proclaimed Tito marshal.
It was Churchill.
And two days later of uprising, Churchill's son, Randolph Churchill, parachuted into Yugoslavia.
And then the rest of it you probably know, somehow, at least some.
From there, well then-- then we waited very restlessly, because of the situation in-- in Hungary.
But to just [INAUDIBLE] coming back to her entering, and to finish with her, then she-- she crossed the [INAUDIBLE] with this company.
And in about three [INAUDIBLE] they were captured.

But then-- then later it was noticeable in the prison already that-- that mission was, I mean, the crossing, the crossing place was already exposed by the-- by the Hungarian counterintelligence military [INAUDIBLE], or by the gendarme, or by-- by-- by-- by somebody.

I will know this, because one of the Canadian Hungarian which was my group, he was on the same prisoner's train from Pec, from southern Hungary, from the [INAUDIBLE] Yugoslav border, where they were shipped up to-- up to Budapest.
And Hannah-- Hannah tried to escape.
But then the books that have been written about her, they even called her a coward and so on, that she wanted to commit suicide.
That's why she jumped the train.
But like hell.
She jumped the train to try to escape.
She had courage.
She had guts.
But they captured her.
There was one, who the Hungarian comrade, who was on the same train with her, on the prison train.
Well, they captured her.
Oh yes, yes, yes, yes, yes.
Well he will say he-- he was-- he was also captured and same-- same.
It [? wasn't ?] before the time, was also in the same prison, but in a prison he never-- they never met.
Because she was in a woman's prison for a while.
And from there she was taken into the same prison where I was, because there was a chief interrogation, the military counterintelligence, and the Gestapo.
So they always transported us.
And she was [INAUDIBLE].
And for the execution place, it was also the [INAUDIBLE] where the execution took place.
Oh, no, no, no, no.
I just-- just-- just found out-- found out later when I went back to Hungary and started to dig.
I was back seven times in Hungary.
And I found Marko [?
Diako ?] that the Hungarian-- Hungarian star, stage star, and then film star, who was close.
Because her father was a-- a-- a-- a-- a scriptwriter.
How-- how-- how do you call it?
Yeah.
And well, they had these friends.
And I-- I-- I met another one, an actress.
And I was filled in about the whole-- about the whole family, and so on, and about-- about Hannah herself.
And I have pictures here which-- which her, oh just a moment.
Ivan Szenes.
Ivan Szenes was her uncle.
Yeah.
I have a picture-- picture with her.
And [INAUDIBLE].
No, no.
With a-- with a friend, friend who [INAUDIBLE] took me around to little [INAUDIBLE] in-- in-- in-- in-- Budapest.
But I visited because I was in television several times in Budapest, starting in 19-- 1980.
And that at one time for the interview, they wanted in an interview like you, but not as much.
Not as much.
But [INAUDIBLE] what was jammed into 10 minutes.
But I couldn't avoid mentioning Hannah Szenes.
[INAUDIBLE] went around with it.
[INAUDIBLE] mentioned Hannah Szenes, the whole Budapest [INAUDIBLE] my God.
Everybody looked around and looked at each other.
Who's this man who-- Hannah Szenes?
How, how come?
Because they knew about her-- especially the Jewish community.
And so how is it this man?
And mind you, there was some-- some incidents with the-- with the Israel.
So that's why I could never go-- never go there, because I was one.
Don't-- don't go there, because they hate you there.
Well not-- not as much as I-- as I expected.
Not as much.
So somehow the--  somehow the-- the-- the Jewish community was split on this-- on this question on Wallenberg and Hannah Szenes.
But she has a-- a plaque in the-- in the prison where they destroyed it, when the Socialist government came, because it was so infamous.
And for a long time they had to destroy it.
But there is a huge plaque for Hannah Szenes.
That this is the place where.
And in the-- in the military-- military-- military history museum.
And when I visited there, on my research and so on, and where I went.
And they took me into the manager's office and told them of my story.
And when I started to talk about-- talk about.
And then in the middle, this when I get there and mentioned Hannah Szenes's name, they just looked at each other.
And somehow he winked.
I had noticed that.
So something's-- something's happened here.
And everybody kept quiet.
And then when I talked and talked.
And they then all right, let's go [INAUDIBLE] show you the-- show you the-- the-- the military museum.
And when it came to the-- when it came to the point, I was walking ahead, you see the man behind me.
And then he just slowed down.
And I took another step forward.
And all the [INAUDIBLE] who came [INAUDIBLE] just came.
And right there she was.
[CRYING] In a glass.
In a glass with a brick wall built up.
That was the wall of the chapel of the prison.
That's where they carried out the-- the executions.
And that was the-- we stood at that wall, that she was her back against it, she's in front of-- front of it, and [INAUDIBLE] behind her they have a huge picture of her.
When I saw that, I just passed out.
Here she is.
That's-- that's why they maneuvered me to the-- wanted to see her.
And that's how it happened.
Coming to 23.
And not accidentally, November the 7, 1944.
November the 7th.
Not accident.
You know the day.
He-- he come to it.
And they knew, because she was betrayed by the Jew who lives in [?
Taranto ?].
[INAUDIBLE] I was just coming to the [INAUDIBLE] so on.
[INAUDIBLE]
Because he was-- he was planted in my-- a month later he was planted in my cell to get information from.
That you wanted to this on a-- on a story?
[PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] Well-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] It was in November.
